dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 respond to a priority 1 medical - sick person at the Family Dollar store on Broad Street.
Hello, I'm Alex with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what made you call 911 today?
I've been having chest pain and weakness for about two weeks. It didn't improve at MCV yesterday.
Mr. Richards, do you know your name?
It's James Richards.
Can you tell me today's date and the year?
Today is Saturday, July 12, 2019.
Do you know where you are now?
I'm at the Family Dollar on Broad Street.
What made you call 911 just now, Mr. Richards?
The pain in my chest got impossible to ignore today.
Mr. Richards, please allow us to quickly assess your airway.
Please proceed.
Your airway is clear and patent. We'll proceed.
Your breathing is 22 breaths per minute and unlabored right now.
Your pulse is 77 beats per minute - it's strong and regular.
Your blood pressure is 152 over 73.
Oxygen saturation is 94% on room air.
Glucose levels couldn't be read at this time.
Let me recount your respirations - 22 per minute.
Mr. Richards, let's go over your mental status again - can you tell me your name and today's date?
My name is James Richards. Today is Saturday, July 12, 2019.
How would you describe the chest pain - what triggers it or makes it worse?
Dull, non-radiating, and hasn't changed all week. Nothing makes it better or worse.
We noted you have a history of COPD, congestive heart failure, and a previous heart attack. Also, a pacemaker?
Yes, and I have bradycardia and an old heart attack - all for years now.
Quick question - would you mind showing me an ID or insurance card?
Let me find his ID - he was sitting in the aisle.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how severe is your pain now?
It's an 8.
May I check your chest and torso for tenderness or abnormal movements?
Yes, go ahead.
Let's hook you up to the cardiac monitor. It's a quick and simple process.
That's fine.
We attached four leads and are beginning continuous monitoring.
A 12-lead EKG shows a paced rhythm with non-specific ST and T wave changes.
I see you have a history with your pacemaker. Have you noticed any issues with it recently?
No, it's been working fine for years.
Let's reposition you for comfort and safety.
Would a dose of aspirin help with the chest discomfort?
Yes, please give it to me.
Administered 324mg of aspirin - please let us know if it helps.
Your pulse increased to 79 and your breathing is now 38, which is somewhat irregular.
SpO₂ is now 96% and your blood pressure is 159 over 61.
Later readings are 74, 14 breaths per minute, and a blood pressure of 151 over 85.
Based on your history and current 12-lead findings, we are following the chest pain protocol.
You mentioned a preference for [COMPANY_NAME] Hospital. Do you mind if we take you there?
Yes, my cardiologist is there. That's where I'd like to go.
Everything is cleared for transport. Mr. Richards is stable and ready.
Pulse is 78 and 15 breaths per minute with a stable rhythm.
Blood pressure is 171 over 78 with SpO₂ reading 94%.
Mr. Richards is now secured on the stretcher in the ambulance.
Do you have any new symptoms or unresolved concerns while we are en route?
I suddenly feel very hot - it's odd considering the air is cool.
Mr. Richards is stable, still alert and oriented. Transport will be smooth and safe.
Mr. Richards, can you look at me and squeeze my hand when you're ready?
(Squeezes hand)
What's the year we're in?
Two thousand nineteen.
Good. You're alert and oriented.
Mr. Richards, do you have any difficulty breathing or feeling like something's stuck in your throat?
No, my airway is completely clear, and I've had no trouble breathing.
I'm going to lift your chin slightly to get a better look. No resistance or sound from your airway.
I've looked at your chest rise and movements. They're symmetrical and not labored - no cyanosis noted.
Could you take a deep breath and hold it for a few seconds?
(Takes a deep breath and holds)
No wheezing or abnormal sounds. Your trachea appears centered.
Your pulse is strong in your wrist and neck. I can feel the rhythmic beats with no irregularities.
Tell me, do you often forget to drink much water or stay hydrated?
That's pretty rare for me, I usually drink a lot.
Blood pressure is normal, right now I read a systolic of 152 and diastolic of 73.
Just confirming, your oxygen level remains at 94% - no sign of hypoxia.
I wasn't able to confirm a blood sugar level with the glucometer here.
I'm double-checking your breathing. I now get around 14 respirations per minute.
How has the weakness and chest discomfort affected your routine lately?
I've had trouble getting off the couch or walking to the bathroom - each day is worse than the last.
I see on your record you are allergic to penicillin.
Have you had any adverse reactions with any medications recently?
No, but I like to be careful because I've had reactions from it in the past.
I note you're not able to give the exact meds you were taking - can you confirm anything you regularly use?
I have heart-related pills for arrhythmias and COPD. I never could recall the names off the top of my head.
Quick question-when did you last have something to eat or drink besides water?
Two days ago. I've been trying to rest and not really eat.
What exactly was going on when you first noticed the chest pain?
I was resting, just reading the paper in the morning. I first felt pressure like a weight had landed on me.
I've observed on our scale you rated the pain of an 8. Any noticeable variations in that intensity today?
Yes, earlier it was even more - like a ten. But it's dropped now as the day goes on.
I'm checking for any skin changes - rashes, hives, jaundice, or color changes that could suggest complications of your COPD or heart disease.
I've had some minor rashes from sitting in one place too long, but skin looks clear otherwise.
You have no obvious rashes, bites, or marks. Normal skin texture and turgor seen.
Let's have a look at your eyes and head. When I shine a light in each of your eyes, can you tell me if I see your pupils constrict?
Yes, both seem to be reacting fine.
Good - symmetrical pupils of equal size and reactive to light observed.
Do you have any discomfort in your throat or nose?
No, I've not felt any real changes in my throat or nasal area.
I'll listen for any abnormal sounds - no indicated issues in this area noted.
I'm palpating your chest for any asymmetrical rise or any abnormal pulse points.
EKG lead placement has not disturbed your comfort - please stay still.
Let me check our updated vitals again.
Pulse: 78, respirations: 15, blood pressure: 171 over 78, SpO₂: 94%.
The aspirin has taken a bit of time - and it doesn't appear to have reduced the pain.
I guess it was a standard reaction - not much relief, but I'm grateful it was given.
Still an 8 for pain, right? Any new changes to report?
It's still an 8. No real change, even after aspirin.
Hospital en route. Any chance of falling asleep or drowsiness in the next hour?
No, I don't get sleepy once I'm upright like this - been that way for ages.
Mr. Richards is a 56-year-old male presenting with chest pain and weakness. GCS is 15.
Vital signs included a pulse of 77, 22 breaths, blood pressure 152/73, saturation 94% on arrival.
Aspirin given, 12-lead EKG performed with a paced rhythm. Transport was stable.
Mr. Richards was transported to [CITY] Doctors' Forest Hospital as requested.
